senses.gloss: the bulk (of), the majority (of); the lion's share

examples.text: مَا لَا يُدْرَكُ كُلُّهُ، لَا يُتْرَكُ جُلُّهُ.
examples.english: What is not wholly achieved is not to be largely abandoned.

examples.text:
جل الناسِ أعداءُ على السرَّاءِ والضَّرَّا
فلا يغرُرْكَ مبتسمٌ وإن أبدى لكَ البشرا
ففي الصدر حزازاتٌ تكادُ تمزقُ الصدرا
ولو كادوا النجومَ هوتْ من الخضراءِ للغبرا
وما الدنيا إذا فكر تَ غير جهنمَ الصُّغرى
ألستَ ترى بها أمماً وكلٌّ تلعنُ الأخرى
لقد جرَّبْتُ جُلَّهُمُ فلمْ أر فيهمُ خيرا
examples.english:
Most men are rabid foes, / Be they in joy or woe!
Do not be fooled by smiles, / Even if seeming kind:
In their breasts is hateful fury, / almost tearing their hearts apart!
If men could plot against the stars, / The stars would fall into the mire.
Life, if you reflect, / Is but a "lesser Hell":
Do you not see in it many nations? / And each of them execrates the other!
I have appraised most sorts and kinds, / And in all, I saw no goodness!

examples.text: ومن السوس: الأُتْرُجُّ ودُهن البنفسج والشاه سبرم والجِلال والبراذع.
examples.english: From Sūs one gets citrons, violet-oil, basil, and horse-covers and pack-saddles.

examples.text: وقد لبسوا التلاليس وجِلال الدوابّ وجعلوا فوق رءوسهم التراب والتبن.
examples.english: They were clothed in sacks and pelts of domestic beasts and had their heads covered with earth and straw.

etymology.text:
Compare Hebrew גַּל (gall).

Borrowed from Classical Persian گُل (gul). Doublet of وَرْد (ward).

Borrowed from Iranian. Compare Central Kurdish جِل (cil, “beggar’s clothes”), Classical Persian جُلّ (jull, “horse-covering, saddle-cloth”), Balti [script needed] (jol, “horse cover”), Yaghnobi [script needed] (jul, “horse-cloth”), Gurani [script needed] (jal, jul, “cover, cloth”). Doublet of شَلَّاق (šallāq), جُوَالَق (juwālaq), and جُوَال (juwāl).

body.text: : see 1, in six places.       -A2-  Also, inf. n.
closed his eyes, and then opened them, in order to see more clearly.
like the hawk looking at the prey.
is also mentioned (in Har p. 161), on the authority of Mtr, as signifying
meaning “ the first of the horses in a race; ” but as being not known in this sense on any other authority.]
Clear, unobscured, exposed to view, displayed, laid open, disclosed,
plainly apparent, overt, conspicuous, manifest, notorious, plain, obvious,
thus used has not been heard. (Er-Rághib, TA.) It is applied as an epithet to information, or tidings, (Msb, TA,)
